en.extract:
The Yunnan nuthatch (Sitta yunnanensis) is a bird species in the nuthatch family Sittidae. It was first described by William Robert Ogilvie-Grant in 1900 based on a male holotype. It is a small nuthatch, measuring 12 centimetres (4.7 in) in length on average and weighs between 7.5 and 13 grams (0.26 and 0.46 oz). The grey-blue upperparts contrast with the light, smooth, buffy lowerparts. It has a fine white eyebrow above a black eyestripe, which is distinct when the plumage is fresh, and exhibits a small degree of sexual dimorphism. A noisy bird, it produces simple, nasal sounds, sometimes in repetitive series. 
Its ecology is not well known, but it feeds on insects that it finds on pine branches. It can be found in the prefectures of Yunnan, Sichuan, and Guizhou in Southwest China, where it prefers sparsely forested pine forests and avoids dense spruce and fir forests. It is generally rare but can be locally common. It has a small range of 170,000 square kilometres (66,000 sq mi). A 2009 study predicted that its population could decrease by 43.6–47.7% between 2040 and 2069.


Taxonomy
The Yunnan nuthatch was first described as Sitta yunnanensis by Scottish ornithologist William Robert Ogilvie-Grant in 1900 based on a male holotype brought from southern Yunnan by Captain Alfred Wingate. It was placed in the subgenus Micrositta by Buturlin in 1916. There are no subspecies. 
The canadensis group, which is also sometimes called the subgenus Micrositta, contains six species; the Yunnan nuthatch, the red-breasted nuthatch (S. canadensis), the Chinese nuthatch (S. villosa), the Corsican nuthatch (S. whiteheadi), Krüper's nuthatch (S. krueperi) and the Algerian nuthatch (S. ledanti). In 1953, the Dutch ornithologists Karel Voous and John G. van Marle thought that the Yunnan nuthatch formed a link between the canadensis and europaea groups, and simultaneously that it was very closely related to the canadensis group, of which it was a basal representative in terms of distribution and plumage. In 1957, Charles Vaurie postulated that the white-tailed nuthatch was the Yunnan nuthatch's closest relative. In 1998, Eric Pasquet studied the mitochondrial DNA about ten species of nuthatch, including the different species in the canadensis group, but the Yunnan nuthatch was not included in the study. In 2014, Éric Pasquet and colleagues published a nuclear and mitochondrial DNA-based phylogeny of 21 species of nuthatch and confirmed the 1998 study relationships within the canadensis group, adding the Yunnan nuthatch, which is found to be the most basal species of the group.


Description

The upperparts of the Yunnan nuthatch are blue-grey, including the calotte, despite being separated from the upper mantle by a paler area. It has a thin white eyebrow, which extends to the forehead and surmounts a black eyestripe, which widens noticeably on the back, on the sides of the mantle. The eye has a fine white circle around it, and it has a white cheek and throat. The underparts are plain and pale, pinkish buff. The beak is thin and pointed and the almost straight culmen can give the impression that the tip of the beak is coming up. Its irises are dark brown, the bill grey-black with the base of the lower mandible horny or yellowish, and the legs and feet are grey-brown. The Yunnan nuthatch is a small nuthatch, measuring around 12 cm (4.7 in) in length on average, and its wing chord is 69.5 to 74 millimetres (2.74 to 2.91 in) on male specimens and 67 to 74 millimetres (2.6 to 2.9 in) on female specimens. On males, the tale measures 35 to 41 millimetres (1.4 to 1.6 in) and on females, it measures between 36 and 38 millimetres (1.4 and 1.5 in).
The species exhibits little sexual dimorphism, but the black of the female's eyestripe is on average less intense and its underparts are duller and greyer. The plumage is fresh from August, and gradually wears down until the following spring. In worn plumage, the whitish tips of the eyebrow feathers are worn away, and the eyebrow line becomes discontinuous or inconspicuous. The upperparts are duller, less blue. The tail and wing feathers also wear out, but the blue-grey tips of the rectrices persist at least until May. The underparts become duller and dirtier, turning greyish-white. The young is duller than the adult, with the eyebrow less marked and not extending to the forehead, sometimes almost absent and consisting of a lighter cap margin. The eye line is also less wide, the cheek is dirty grey and not white. The throat is whiter, but the rest of the underparts are duller, greyish cinnamon, though not as pale as in a worn plumaged adult. The upperparts are duller and greyer. The bill of fledged young is shorter and has a pale base. Adults undergo a complete moult from July to September, and perhaps sometimes a partial moult before the breeding season in January and February, which includes the breast.
The giant nuthatch can occur in the same type of habitat as the Yunnan nuthatch, but is much larger in size and lacks a white eyebrow. The chestnut-vented nuthatch is closer in size to the Yunnan nuthatch, but has reddish flanks and no white eyebrows.


Ecology and behaviour


Vocalizations

The Yunnan nuthatch is a fairly noisy bird, and typically makes many individual sounds: nit, kni, tit, pit, or a low and nasal toik. Its call, nit, is repeated in sets lasting four to ten seconds of five to six repetitions per second, producing a kni-kni-kni, kit-kit-kit, pi-pi-pi, or a nasal niew-niew-niew sound. It also produces a harsh schri-schri-schri or szi-szi-szi call, a trait shared by other small nuthatches. It sometimes produces a ziew-ziew-ziew call in calm sets of three notes per second, and can also emit nasal calls quit-quit-quit, schu-schu-schu, or tui-tui-tui.


Food and breeding
The diet of the Yunnan nuthatch is not well-known, but it is known to consume insects it catches on pine branches. There is a lack of information about its reproduction, but a female that was collected on 9 March was close to laying, while recently born Yunnan nuthatches were collected on 21 May.


Distribution and habitat

The Yunnan nuthatch is endemic to Southwest China, and mainly occurs in the prefectures of Yunnan, Sichuan, and Guizhou. A study published in 2003 identified the species being endemic to China, and identified the Hengduan Mountains as the main area of endemism, but the bird also occurs in the Anjaw district of Arunachal Pradesh in India.
The Yunnan nuthatch is a sedentary species. It inhabits pine forests with sparse undergrowth, and avoids dense spruce and fir forests. Occasionally, it occurs on small pine trees 2 to 3 m (6.6 to 9.8 ft) high, in open forests or among scattered groups of trees. In the summer, it lives at altitudes between 2,440 and 3,960 m (8,010 and 12,990 ft) above sea level (ASL), and travels down into valleys during winter to heights up to 1,200 m (3,900 ft). However, it has been observed at between 2,600 and 4,000 m (8,500 and 13,100 ft) between November and January in Shuangbai County.


Threats and protection
In 1987, Chinese ornithologist Zheng Zuoxin described the Yunnan nuthatch as rare, but it is locally common in areas such as Lijiang's pine forests. It has a small range of around 170,000 square kilometres (66,000 sq mi), and the bird disappeared from several locations in the early twentieth century. It is threatened by habitat destruction and is dependent on old pine forests, but also appears to live in degraded habitats. A 2009 study attempted to predict the impact of climate change on nuthatches in Asia, and shows the Yunnan nuthatch's distribution decreasing by between 43.6% and 47.7% by 2040–2069. The species used to be listed as near-threatened species by the International Union for Conservation of Nature, but is classified as least concern as of 2023.


References

The '''Yunnan nuthatch''' ('''''Sitta yunnanensis''''') is a bird [[species]] in the nuthatch family [[Sittidae]]. It was first described by [[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant]] in 1900 based on a male [[holotype]]. It is a small nuthatch, measuring {{convert|12|cm}} in length on average and weighs between {{convert|7.5|and|13|g}}. The grey-blue [[upperparts]] contrast with the light, smooth, buffy lowerparts. It has a fine white eyebrow above a black eyestripe, which is distinct when the plumage is fresh, and exhibits a small degree of [[sexual dimorphism]]. A noisy bird, it produces simple, nasal sounds, sometimes in repetitive series. 

Its ecology is not well known, but it feeds on insects that it finds on pine branches. It can be found in the [[prefectures]] of [[Yunnan]], [[Sichuan]], and [[Guizhou]] in [[Southwest China]], where it prefers sparsely forested pine forests and avoids dense [[spruce]] and [[fir]] forests. It is generally rare but can be locally common. It has a small range of {{convert|170000|sqkm}}. A 2009 study predicted that its population could decrease by 43.6–47.7% between 2040 and 2069.

==Taxonomy==
The Yunnan nuthatch was first [[Species description|described]] as ''Sitta yunnanensis'' by Scottish [[Ornithology|ornithologist]] [[William Robert Ogilvie-Grant]] in 1900 based on a male [[holotype]] brought from southern [[Yunnan]] by [[Captain]] Alfred Wingate.<ref name="Ogilvie-Grant">{{cite book|title=New species from southern China Collected by Capt. Wingate AMS|url=https://archive.org/details/bulletino101518991905brit/page/n107/mode/2up?view=theater|publisher=[[Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club]]|volume=10|year=1900|page=37}}</ref> It was placed in the subgenus ''Micrositta'' by Buturlin in 1916.<ref name="Micrositta">{{cite book|last=Matthysen|first=Erik|title=The Nuthatches|publisher=[[A & C Black]]|year=2010|isbn=978-1-4081-2870-1|pages=269–270|language=en|chapter=Appendix I ("Scientific and Common Names of Nuthatches")}}</ref> There are no subspecies.<ref name="WBL">{{cite web|url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/nuthatch/|title=Nuthatches, Wallcreeper, treecreepers, mockingbirds, starlings & oxpeckers|date=22 October 2016|publisher=International Ornithological Congress (IOC) World Bird List|series=Version 6.4|at=Family Sittidae|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161222041438/http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/nuthatch/|url-status=live|archive-date=22 December 2016}}</ref><ref name="AlanPP">{{cite web |url=http://www.zoonomen.net/avtax/pa48.html |title=World Birds Taxonomic List: Genera and species with citations |author=Alan P. Peterson|author-link=Alan P. Peterson|publisher=Zoological Nomenclature Resource |date=18 August 2014|access-date=2 January 2015}}</ref> 

The ''canadensis'' group, which is also sometimes called the subgenus ''Micrositta'',<ref name="Micrositta"/> contains six species; the Yunnan nuthatch, the [[red-breasted nuthatch]] (''S. canadensis''), the [[Chinese nuthatch]] (''S. villosa''), the [[Corsican nuthatch]] (''S. whiteheadi''), [[Krüper's nuthatch]] (''S. krueperi'') and the [[Algerian nuthatch]] (''S. ledanti''). In 1953, the Dutch ornithologists [[Karel Voous]] and John G. van Marle thought that the Yunnan nuthatch formed a link between the ''canadensis'' and ''europaea'' groups, and simultaneously that it was very closely related to the canadensis group, of which it was a basal representative in terms of distribution and plumage.<ref name="Harrap p. 144">{{cite book|last=Harrap|first=Simon|title=Chickadees, Tits, Nuthatches and Treecreepers|date=1995|publisher=[[Princeton University Press]]|others=Illustrated by David Quinn|isbn=0-691-01083-8|editor=[[Christopher Helm]]|pages=143–144|language=en}}</ref> In 1957, [[Charles Vaurie]] postulated that the white-tailed nuthatch was the Yunnan nuthatch's closest relative.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://digitallibrary.amnh.org/bitstream/handle/2246/3596//v2/dspace/ingest/pdfSource/nov/N1854.pdf?isAllowed=y&sequence=1|title=Systematic Notes on Palearctic Birds |publisher=digitallibrary.amnh.org |date=November 29, 1957 |access-date=2020-05-19}}</ref> In 1998, Eric Pasquet studied the [[mitochondrial DNA]] about ten species of nuthatch, including the different species in the ''canadensis'' group, but the Yunnan nuthatch was not included in the study.<ref>{{Cite journal|url=https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/abs/10.1111/j.1474-919X.1998.tb04553.x|title=Phylogeny of the nuthatches of the Sitta canadensis group and its evolutionary and biogeographic implications|first=Eric|last=Pasquet|date=May 19, 1998|journal=[[Ibis (journal)|Ibis]]|volume=140|issue=1|pages=150–156|via=Wiley Online Library|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1998.tb04553.x|url-access=subscription}}</ref> In 2014, Éric Pasquet and colleagues published a [[Nuclear DNA|nuclear]] and mitochondrial DNA-based [[phylogeny]] of 21 species of nuthatch and confirmed the 1998 study relationships within the ''canadensis'' group, adding the Yunnan nuthatch, which is found to be the most [[Basal (phylogenetics)|basal]] species of the group.<ref name="EPasquet2014">{{cite journal|doi=10.1007/s10336-014-1063-7|url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10336-014-1063-7|title=Evolution within the nuthatches (Sittidae: Aves, Passeriformes): molecular phylogeny, biogeography, and ecological perspectives|journal=[[Journal of Ornithology]]|date=April 2014|author1=Eric Pasquet |author2=Keith F. Barker |author3=Jochen Martens |author4=Annie Tillier |author5=Corinne Cruaud |author6=Alice Cibois|volume=155|issue=3|pages=755–765|bibcode=2014JOrni.155..755P |s2cid=17637707|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

==Description==
[[File:SittaYunnanensis.svg|thumb|alt=Digital drawing of a bird with grey upperparts, whitish underparts, and a black eyestripe|Schematic representation of the Yunnan nuthatch in fresh plumage.]]
The {{birdgloss|upperparts}} of the Yunnan nuthatch are blue-grey, including the [[wikt:calotte#Noun|calotte]], despite being separated from the upper [[Glossary of bird terms#mantle|mantle]] by a paler area. It has a thin white eyebrow, which extends to the forehead and surmounts a black eyestripe, which widens noticeably on the back, on the sides of the mantle. The eye has a fine white circle around it, and it has a white cheek and throat. The {{birdgloss|underparts}} are plain and pale, pinkish buff. The [[beak]] is thin and pointed and the almost straight [[Beak#Culmen|culmen]] can give the impression that the tip of the beak is coming up.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> Its [[Iris (anatomy)|iris]]es are dark brown, the bill grey-black with the base of the lower mandible horny or yellowish, and the legs and feet are grey-brown.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> The Yunnan nuthatch is a small nuthatch, measuring around {{convert|12|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length on average,<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> and its [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] is {{convert|69.5|to|74|mm}} on male specimens and {{convert|67|to|74|mm}} on female specimens. On males, the tale measures {{convert|35|to|41|mm}} and on females, it measures between {{convert|36|and|38|mm}}.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/>

The species exhibits little [[sexual dimorphism]], but the black of the female's eyestripe is on average less intense and its underparts are duller and greyer. The [[plumage]] is fresh from August, and gradually wears down until the following spring.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> In worn plumage, the whitish tips of the eyebrow feathers are worn away, and the eyebrow line becomes discontinuous or inconspicuous. The upperparts are duller, less blue. The tail and wing feathers also wear out, but the blue-grey tips of the [[rectrices]] persist at least until May. The underparts become duller and dirtier, turning greyish-white.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> The young is duller than the adult, with the eyebrow less marked and not extending to the forehead, sometimes almost absent and consisting of a lighter cap margin. The eye line is also less wide, the cheek is dirty grey and not white. The throat is whiter, but the rest of the underparts are duller, greyish cinnamon, though not as pale as in a worn plumaged adult.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> The upperparts are duller and greyer.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> The bill of [[fledge]]d young is shorter and has a pale base.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> Adults undergo a complete [[moulting|moult]] from July to September, and perhaps sometimes a partial moult before the breeding season in January and February, which includes the breast.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/>

The [[giant nuthatch]] can occur in the same type of habitat as the Yunnan nuthatch, but is much larger in size and lacks a white eyebrow. The [[chestnut-vented nuthatch]] is closer in size to the Yunnan nuthatch, but has reddish flanks and no white eyebrows.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/>

==Ecology and behaviour==

===Vocalizations===
{{Birdsong|species=''Sitta yunnanensis''|url=http://www.xeno-canto.org/species/Sitta-yunnanensis}}
The Yunnan nuthatch is a fairly noisy bird, and typically makes many individual sounds: ''nit'', ''{{not a typo|kni}}'', ''tit'', ''pit'', or a low and nasal ''{{not a typo|toik}}''. Its call, ''nit'', is repeated in sets lasting four to ten seconds of five to six repetitions per second, producing a ''{{not a typo|kni-kni-kni}}'', ''{{not a typo|kit-kit-kit}}'', ''{{not a typo|pi-pi-pi}}'', or a nasal ''{{not a typo|niew-niew-niew}}'' sound.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> It also produces a harsh ''{{not a typo|schri-schri-schri}}'' or ''{{not a typo|szi-szi-szi}}'' call, a trait shared by other small nuthatches. It sometimes produces a ''{{not a typo|ziew-ziew-ziew}}'' call in calm sets of three notes per second, and can also emit nasal calls ''{{not a typo|quit-quit-quit}}'', ''{{not a typo|schu-schu-schu}}'', or ''{{not a typo|tui-tui-tui}}''.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/>

===Food and breeding===
The diet of the Yunnan nuthatch is not well-known, but it is known to consume [[insects]] it catches on pine branches. There is a lack of information about its reproduction, but a female that was collected on 9 March was close to laying, while recently born Yunnan nuthatches were collected on 21 May.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/><ref name="Hoyo">{{cite book |first1=Josep |last1=del Hoyo |first2=Andrew |last2=Elliott |first3=David A. |last3=Christie|title=[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]] |volume=13: Penduline-tits to Shrikes|publisher=[[Lynx Edicions]] |year=2008|page=136}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
[[File:Pinus yunnanensis Yulongxueshan.jpg|thumb|alt=Snow-capped mountains with pine trees in the foreground|View of the snow-covered [[Jade Dragon Snow Mountain]], with ''[[Pinus yunnanensis]]'' pines in the foreground.]]
The Yunnan nuthatch is endemic to [[Southwest China]], and mainly occurs in the [[prefectures]] of Yunnan, [[Sichuan]], and [[Guizhou]].<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> A study published in 2003 identified the species being [[Endemism|endemic]] to China, and identified the [[Hengduan Mountains]] as the main area of endemism, but the bird also occurs in the Anjaw district of Arunachal Pradesh in India.<ref name="2003China">{{cite journal|first1=Lei|last1=Fu-Min|first2=Qu|last2=Yan-Hua|first3=Lu|last3=Jian-Li|first4=Liu|last4=Yao|first5=Yin|last5=Zuo-Hua|title=Conservation on diversity and distribution patterns of endemic birds in China|url=https://link.springer.com/article/10.1023/A:1021928801558|journal=Biodiversity & Conservation|volume=12|issue=2|date=February 2003|doi=10.1023/A:1021928801558|pages=239–254|bibcode=2003BiCon..12..239L |s2cid=24390990|url-access=subscription}}</ref>

The Yunnan nuthatch is a [[sedentism|sedentary]] species. It inhabits [[pine]] forests with sparse undergrowth, and avoids dense [[spruce]] and [[fir]] forests. Occasionally, it occurs on small pine trees {{convert|2|to|3|m|ft|abbr=on}} high, in open forests or among scattered groups of trees.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> In the summer, it lives at altitudes between {{convert|2440|and|3960|m|ft|abbr=on}} above [[sea level]] (ASL), and travels down into valleys during winter to heights up to {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}}. However, it has been observed at between {{convert|2600|and|4000|m|ft|abbr=on}} between November and January in [[Shuangbai County]].<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/>

== Threats and protection ==
In 1987, Chinese ornithologist [[Zheng Zuoxin]] described the Yunnan nuthatch as rare,<ref name="ZuoxinZheng">{{cite book |title=A synopsis of the avifauna of China|url=https://www.nhbs.com/a-synopsis-of-the-avifauna-of-china-book|publisher=[[Science Press]] |location=Pékin |year=1987|isbn=978-3-490-12518-7}}</ref> but it is locally common in areas such as [[Lijiang]]'s pine forests. It has a small range of around {{convert|170000|sqkm}},<ref name="BirdLife">{{cite web |url=http://datazone.birdlife.org/species/factsheet/22711192 |title=Yunnan nathatch |publisher=[[BirdLife]] |access-date=2 January 2015}}</ref> and the bird disappeared from several locations in the early twentieth century.<ref name="Hoyo"/> It is threatened by [[habitat destruction]] and is dependent on old pine forests, but also appears to live in degraded habitats.<ref name="Harrap p. 144"/> A 2009 study attempted to predict the [[effects of climate change|impact of climate change]] on nuthatches in Asia, and shows the Yunnan nuthatch's distribution decreasing by between 43.6% and 47.7% by 2040–2069.<ref name="Menon">{{cite book |first=Shaily |last=Menon |title=Projected climate change effects on nuthatch distribution and diversity across Asia |publisher=[[The Raffles Bulletin of Zoology]] |volume=57|issue=2|date=August 2003 |pages=569–575 |url=http://scholarworks.gvsu.edu/cgi/viewcontent.cgi?article=1005&context=biopeerpubs}}</ref> The species used to be listed as [[near-threatened species]] by the [[International Union for Conservation of Nature]], but is classified as least concern as of 2023.<ref name="iucn status 06 July 2023"/>

zh.extract:
滇鳾（學名：Sitta yunnanensis），又名雲南鳾，是雀形目鳾科的一種，生活于中國西南部。种加词 yunnanensis 意为“云南的”。


生态环境
高原鸟类，生活在海拔1300米－3200米的中山和高山地带，活动于山谷山坡的针叶林或针阔混交林间，有时可见与普通鳾混群活动。


分布地域
滇鳾为中国特有鸟种，分布于中国西南隅的三江并流区附近的山区，包括西藏东南部，四川西部、和云南西北部地区。


特征
滇鳾雄雌同形同色，成鸟上体为兰灰色，具粗大而长的黑色贯眼纹，贯眼纹甚长，可达肩部，贯眼纹上方具细的白色眉纹，这道细细的白色眉纹是其他鳾属鸟类所不具有的，是滇鳾的鉴别特征，另外滇鳾的下体为极淡的灰色，近污白色也是在野外识别滇鳾的一个重要特征。尾羽大部分为与上背相同的蓝灰色，但次外侧尾羽为黑色。虹膜暗褐色，喙黑色，足灰色。在野外，辨识本物种除了依靠上述形体特征外，还须参考其行为，鳾属鸟类大多喜欢在粗糙的树皮表面上下攀爬，常头上脚下地栖息，这种倒挂金钟的状态是其他鸟类少见的。


食物
滇鳾攀爬树皮以藏身于树皮缝隙内的昆虫为主要食物，主要取食鳞翅目、鞘翅目昆虫的幼虫，除了动物性食物，偶尔也取食花蕊、松子和其他植物的果实。


繁殖与保护
繁殖不详。

IUCN濒危等级：LR/nt（生效年代： 2003年）


參考

BirdLife International. Sitta yunnanensis. The IUCN Red List of Threatened Species 2004.   [2007年3月14日] （英语）. 
Sitta yunnanensis. ITIS. 


外部链接
滇鳾观测记录 （页面存档备份，存于互联网档案馆）
